does: This paper introduces the DIQ-H benchmark and a Value-Guided Iterative Refinement method for evaluating Vision-Language Models under adversarial visual conditions. DIQ-H simulates real-world image degradations including motion blur, sensor noise, and compression artifacts applied to sequential visual inputs, measuring how these corruptions trigger hallucinations and misaligned outputs that compound over time. The benchmark explicitly tracks error propagation and value consistency across continuous deployment sequences rather than isolated static images. The work provides systematic evaluation protocols to assess VLM robustness in scenarios relevant to embodied AI and safety-critical applications like robotics and autonomous systems, offering metrics that capture both immediate response quality and temporal coherence under degraded conditions.
connects: This work extends prior research on VLM robustness and hallucination detection by moving beyond static benchmark evaluation toward temporal, adversarial settings. It builds on growing recognition that standard benchmarks fail to capture real-world deployment challenges—particularly adversarial visual conditions and error propagation in continuous systems. The paper responds to limitations in existing VLM evaluation frameworks that either ignore perturbations entirely or treat single frames in isolation, missing cumulative effects. It connects to broader work on adversarial robustness in vision models, hallucination mitigation, and value alignment in AI systems, while addressing the specific challenge of maintaining consistent, trustworthy behavior under realistic environmental stressors relevant to embodied AI deployment.
enables: This work establishes evaluation methodology and benchmarking infrastructure for developing more robust VLMs suited to real-world deployment. By quantifying how visual degradation propagates errors temporally, it enables future research into mechanisms that maintain value consistency and reduce hallucinations under adversarial conditions. The DIQ-H benchmark and refinement approach can guide development of VLMs with enhanced robustness to sensor noise, motion blur, and other natural perturbations. This foundation supports safety-critical applications in robotics and autonomous systems by providing systematic testing protocols and error characterization. The framework also opens directions for investigating architectures and training methods that explicitly model temporal error propagation and maintain aligned reasoning across sequences.
